skill.name: supabase
skill.description: Manage Supabase projects, databases, migrations, Edge Functions, storage, secrets, and type generation with Supabase CLI guidance.
skill.summary: Guide AI agents through common Supabase CLI workflows for local and remote project operations.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown backticks as Ruby shell execution, plus weak cryptography and reconnaissance indicators. Review found these are command examples for the Supabase CLI, not embedded executable code or prompt injection. The skill remains medium risk because it guides agents to run external commands that can alter databases, secrets, storage, and deployments.

medium_findings.title: Supabase CLI Commands Can Modify Cloud and Local Resources
medium_findings.description: The skill provides Supabase CLI examples for linking projects, pushing migrations, resetting databases, deploying functions, changing secrets, and copying storage objects. These are legitimate DevOps operations, but they can alter or expose real project data if run without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain direct Supabase CLI commands for remote links, migrations, deployments, storage, and secrets. The intent is legitimate, but the operational impact is clear and should require user review before execution.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Execution Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The many external command detections come from fenced Bash examples and inline Markdown code spans. They are not Ruby backtick execution in a script, but they still describe commands an agent may run.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows the flagged backticks are Markdown fences around Bash command examples. No executable Ruby file or shell interpolation is present in the skill package.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detection Is a Textual False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography flag at the description line has no supporting crypto operation. The line only describes managing Supabase with the Supabase CLI.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line is metadata text and contains no hashing, encryption, cipher selection, or cryptographic API use. This makes the weak-crypto pattern a scanner mismatch.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detection Is Type Generation Guidance
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance flag points to a Supabase type-generation command using a project identifier. This can query remote schema metadata, but it is expected behavior for Supabase development.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced command generates TypeScript types from a Supabase project schema. It is an external metadata operation, but there is no evidence of host reconnaissance or malicious discovery.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Supabase CLI Invocation Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly instructs use of the supabase command-line tool. This is expected for the skill purpose, but agents should treat these commands as user-approved operations, especially when targeting remote projects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file is a Supabase CLI reference and the command examples are explicit. The pattern is legitimate but operationally sensitive.

content.user_title: Manage Supabase Projects with CLI Guidance
content.value_statement: Supabase teams need consistent commands for local development, migrations, functions, storage, and secrets. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused Supabase CLI reference for common project workflows.
actual_capabilities: Lists Supabase project and project-linking commands.
Covers local stack start, stop, and status checks.
Provides database commands for SQL execution, schema pull, push, reset, and diff.
Documents migration creation, listing, application, and squashing commands.
Includes Edge Function, storage, secret, type generation, and login workflows.
limitations: Does not validate command output or inspect project state by itself.
Requires the Supabase CLI to be installed and authenticated where commands run.
Does not include rollback planning or production change controls.
Provides command examples, not a full Supabase architecture guide.

use_cases.title: Set up a local Supabase stack
use_cases.description: Start, stop, and inspect local Supabase services during application development.

use_cases.title: Prepare database migrations
use_cases.description: Create migrations, compare schemas, and apply changes through the Supabase CLI.

use_cases.title: Operate functions, storage, and secrets
use_cases.description: Find the right commands for Edge Functions, storage transfers, and function secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Start local development
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a local Supabase environment.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the Supabase CLI commands to start the local stack, check service status, and stop it when finished.

prompt_templates.title: Compare database schemas
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow before creating or applying migrations.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me review the Supabase CLI steps to pull the remote schema, compare it with local changes, and create a migration.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy an Edge Function
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for function release preparation.
prompt_templates.prompt: List the Supabase CLI commands to create, serve, inspect logs, and deploy an Edge Function. Include where user confirmation is needed.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a controlled production update
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow across database, functions, storage, and secrets.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a careful Supabase CLI runbook for a production update that includes migrations, Edge Functions, secrets, and storage checks.

output_examples.input: I need to update my local database schema from a Supabase project.
output_examples.output: The skill would identify the project-linking step, then recommend pulling the remote schema and checking differences before applying migrations.

output_examples.input: I want to deploy an Edge Function and review logs.
output_examples.output: The skill would point to function creation, local serving, deployment, and log inspection commands, with a reminder to confirm the target project.

output_examples.input: I need to manage Supabase storage files from the terminal.
output_examples.output: The skill would show the storage list and copy workflows for bucket paths and local files, while noting data transfer risks.

best_practices: Confirm the linked Supabase project before running migration, secret, storage, or deployment commands.
Review generated migrations and schema diffs before applying changes to shared environments.
Use environment-specific credentials and avoid exposing secret values in chat or logs.
anti_patterns: Do not run database reset or push commands against production without an approved change plan.
Do not paste real secret values into prompts unless the environment is approved for sensitive data.
Do not deploy functions or copy storage objects before confirming the target project and bucket.

faq.question: Does this skill run Supabase commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides command guidance for an AI agent. The user should approve commands before execution.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it manage remote Supabase projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes linking, schema, type generation, functions, storage, and secrets commands for remote projects.

faq.question: Is it safe for production use?
faq.answer: It can be used with production projects only when commands are reviewed and approved through normal change controls.

faq.question: Does it include Supabase API code examples?
faq.answer: No. The skill focuses on Supabase CLI workflows, not application client code.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The commands are legitimate, but they can change databases, deployments, secrets, and storage when executed.
